title: Quiz: Cross-Resonance Gates
description: Test your understanding of cross-resonance physics and implementation

questions.question: In a cross-resonance gate, the control qubit is driven at:

options.text: Its own resonance frequency
The frequency of the target qubit
The average of both qubit frequencies
Twice the target qubit frequency

questions.explanation: In a CR gate, the control qubit is driven at the target qubit's frequency. This off-resonant drive, combined with the coupling between qubits, generates an effective conditional rotation on the target qubit that depends on the control qubit state.

questions.question: What is the desired interaction term in the cross-resonance effective Hamiltonian?

options.text: ZZ (σz ⊗ σz) - always-on coupling
ZX (σz ⊗ σx) - conditional X rotation on target
IX (I ⊗ σx) - unconditional X rotation on target
XX (σx ⊗ σx) - simultaneous X rotations

questions.explanation: The ZX term (σz ⊗ σx) is the desired entangling interaction. It causes the target qubit to rotate about X with a direction that depends on the control qubit state: +X rotation when control is |0⟩, -X rotation when control is |1⟩.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the echoed CR sequence (CR+ → X → CR- → X)?

options.text: To double the gate speed
To cancel spurious single-qubit terms (IX, ZI) while preserving the ZX interaction
To eliminate the ZX interaction
To reduce the required drive amplitude

questions.explanation: The echo sequence cancels unwanted single-qubit rotations (IX on target, ZI on control) by having them accumulate with opposite signs in the two CR halves. The desired ZX term adds constructively since it involves both qubits and is preserved by the X echo.

questions.question: How is a CNOT gate constructed from a cross-resonance ZX interaction?

options.text: A single ZX(π) rotation is equivalent to CNOT
ZX(π/2) combined with single-qubit Z and X corrections
Two ZX(π/4) rotations back-to-back
ZX rotations are not sufficient for CNOT

questions.explanation: CNOT can be constructed from ZX(π/2) plus single-qubit corrections: CNOT = (I ⊗ Rx(-π/2)) · ZX(π/2) · (Rz(-π/2) ⊗ I), up to a global phase. The single-qubit gates convert the ZX rotation into the standard CNOT form.

questions.question: What determines the rate of the ZX interaction in a CR gate?

options.text: Only the CR drive amplitude
The coupling strength J, drive amplitude Ω, and frequency detuning Δ: ωZX ∝ J×Ω/Δ
Only the qubit coherence times
The temperature of the dilution refrigerator

questions.explanation: The ZX rate scales as ωZX ∝ J×Ω/Δ where J is the qubit-qubit coupling strength, Ω is the CR drive amplitude, and Δ is the frequency difference between qubits. Larger coupling and smaller detuning give faster gates.

questions.question: What is 'classical crosstalk' in the context of CR gates?

options.text: Electrical interference between control cables
Direct excitation of the target qubit by the CR drive (the IX term)
Thermal noise coupling between qubits
Memory effects in the control electronics

questions.explanation: Classical crosstalk refers to direct excitation of the target qubit by the CR drive field, even without the quantum coupling. This appears as an IX term in the effective Hamiltonian and causes unconditional rotation of the target, which must be canceled by the echo or active cancellation.

questions.question: The echoed CR sequence completely eliminates all error sources, resulting in a perfect two-qubit gate.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. While the echo cancels IX and ZI single-qubit terms, it does not eliminate: (1) ZZ coupling which accumulates during the gate, (2) leakage to higher transmon levels, (3) decoherence during the long gate time, (4) imperfect echo X gates. These residual errors limit CR gate fidelity to ~99-99.5%.

questions.question: Why are CR gates particularly suitable for fixed-frequency transmon systems?

options.text: They are faster than frequency-tuned gates
They require no flux tuning, using only existing microwave drive lines
They achieve higher fidelity than any other two-qubit gate
They don't require any calibration

questions.explanation: CR gates are all-microwave operations that don't require frequency tuning of the qubits. This makes them ideal for fixed-frequency transmons where flux-based frequency tuning would add noise and complexity. The same drive lines used for single-qubit gates can implement CR.
